steps.title: Variables in Scratch — and now in Python

narration.default: In Scratch you use the orange Variables blocks: one to SET a value, and a rounded reporter to USE it. Python does the same two things — but as text. Let's write it.

On line 1, type:

message = "Hello Python!"

Left side = the name (like Scratch's variable name). Equals sign = SET. Right side = the value.

steps.title: Why the terminal is still empty

narration.default: Setting a variable shows nothing on its own — just like clicking the Scratch SET block doesn't say anything aloud. Python remembers the value quietly. Next we'll USE it.

Press Enter for line 2, then type:

print(message)

In Scratch you'd drag the rounded message reporter into a say block. In Python, you just type the name.

No quotes around message — that's important. With quotes ("message") Python would print the WORD message. Without quotes, Python looks up the value the name stands for. The reporter block in Scratch works the same way: it uses the value, not the name.
Click Run. The terminal shows: Hello Python! Python looked up message, found "Hello Python!", and printed that — the same way Scratch's say block reads the reporter.
Change the text on line 1 — your name, a joke, anything. Click Run again. The print line stays the same. SET once, USE many times — that's the whole point of a variable, in Scratch and in Python.
